name: Earl Hines
abstract: Earl Kenneth Hines (December 28, 1903 – April 22, 1983), also known as Earl "Fatha" Hines, was an American jazz pianist and bandleader. He was one of the most influential figures in the development of jazz piano and, according to one source, "one of a small number of pianists whose playing shaped the history of jazz".

about_quotes.speaker: Armand Hug
about_quotes.evidence: the first one that really had a terrific impact on me was Earl Hines. I think that he did more to revolutionize my style of playing probably than any other pianist that I know of.

about_quotes.speaker: Mose Allison
about_quotes.evidence: [INTERVIEWER] Well, Elizabeth Staton had a wind-up Victrola with Earl Hines and...oh, a lot of other people. I listened to that for a while.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mose Allison — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Lizzie Miles
about_quotes.evidence: no I never worked with Earl Hines but I just like his style of playing I just like the way he plays but I never worked with him

about_quotes.speaker: Eddie Locke
about_quotes.evidence: You played with Earl Hines? ... Oh that was something. That “Live at the Vanguard.” ... we played with him.
about_quotes.doc_title: Eddie Locke interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York, NY, January 13, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Norris Turney
about_quotes.evidence: And we used to listen to big bands, Fletcher Henderson, Earl Hines, Basie when he first went to New York
about_quotes.doc_title: Norris Turney interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Etta Jones
about_quotes.evidence: Etta has collaborated with such greats as ... Earl “Fatha” Hines ... both in concert and on recordings.
about_quotes.doc_title: Etta Jones interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, October 2, 1998
